figures.caption: Table 1. Performance on Maximizing Interest across KuaiRec and MovieLens-1M. FlexRec achieves superior single-need ranking performance compared with baselines.
Table 2. Need generalization results (train: Maximizing Interest, test: Explore New Topics) indicate that FlexRec transfers strong ranking abilities effectively across needs.
Table 7. Ablation of reward formulations for GRPO and PPO on the KuaiRec Maximizing Interest task under the dense-interaction setting.
Table 9. Performance on the Maximizing Interest need on dense version of KuaiRec.

Figure 4. Validation NDCG@5 and NDCG@10 during training under different reward signals (UA denotes uncertainty-aware update), showing that a learned critic with uncertainty-aware GRPO achieves the strongest and most stable learning.

Table 3. Performance on Amazon Product Search (BERT4Rec not applicable to non-sequential setting), showing that FlexRec is effective not only for sequential recommendation but also for non-sequential ranking tasks such as product search.
Figure 3. Validation NDCG@5 and NDCG@10 during training under different reward designs, showing that FlexRec’s swap-based item-level reward outperforms sequence-level GRPO.

Figure 1. Overall framework of FlexRec. Given a candidate set and an explicit user need, an LLM recommender generates ranked rollouts. An item-level reward is computed by evaluating the marginal contribution of individual item placements via counterfactual swaps (top right). A critic predicts both the expected interaction reward and its uncertainty for unobserved interactions. These estimates are used to form uncertainty-aware advantages, down-weighting unreliable signals during optimization (bottom).
Table 6. Need generalization results (train: Maximizing Interest, test: Trend Promotion) indicate that FlexRec transfers strong ranking abilities effectively across needs.
